title: Ports & Adapters Flow
subtitle: Watch one business request cross the boundary: adapters translate, the core decides, and technology answers through ports.

panels.heading: Translate intent
panels.body: A driver adapter turns HTTP, CLI, or test input into a use-case command the application understands.
bullets: No framework semantics leak into business rules
The entry contract stays stable even when controllers change

panels.heading: Enter the use case
panels.body: The core receives business intent through a contract that speaks application language, not transport language.
bullets: Validation, policy, orchestration, and decision live here
This is where the system should remain technology-agnostic

panels.heading: Request capability
panels.body: The core asks for persistence, payment, or notification as an ability, not as a specific vendor call.

panels.heading: Bind to technology
panels.body: A driven adapter speaks Postgres, MongoDB, Stripe, or email protocol at the edge so the core does not have to.
bullets: Adapters translate data shape and protocol details

panels.heading: Return outcome
panels.body: The capability result comes back through the port boundary, while business consequence stays inside the core.
bullets: Success or failure becomes domain-level handling
If every change leaks inward, the boundary is fake

footer: Hexagonal Architecture is not about drawing a hexagon. It is about keeping translation at the edge and ownership of decisions in the core.
